perturbation.original_text: CBC undertakes, at its sole cost and expense, to take out and maintain an "All risk" insurance against loss of and destruction or damage to the Facility (including fire, theft and vandalism, etc.), third party liability insurance, product liability insurance for the Bromelain SP and employers liability insurance. Without prejudice to the above, CBC shall maintain, or shall cause to be maintained with respect to itself and each of its Affiliates, such types and levels of insurance (including, without limitation, third party and product liability insurance), as are customary in the pharmaceutical or manufacturing industry to provide coverage for their activities contemplated hereby. Upon request of MediWound, CBC shall keep MediWound informed of the general parameters of its liability insurance program and any proposed substantive changes therein. Upon request, CBC shall furnish MediWound certification of insurance (and/or true copies of policies) showing the above coverage, signed by an authorized agent of the insurance company, certifying that liability assumed under this Agreement is fully insured without exception, and providing for at least thirty (30) days prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: CBC undertakes to obtain insurance coverage deemed adequate by its internal risk assessment. CBC shall maintain insurance customary for the pharmaceutical or manufacturing industry. Upon request, CBC may provide MediWound with a summary of its insurance coverage.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates specific insurance types and requires certification of full insurance coverage. The modification weakens this requirement, making it subjective and discretionary. Removing the 30-day prior written notice reduces MediWound's ability to monitor compliance. Since New York law governs, this omission conflicts with requirements to maintain adequate insurance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts NY Ins. Law § 2102 because it removes the requirement for CBC to maintain specific types and levels of insurance and provide certification, undermining MediWound's protection and potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.

2025-03-28 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2102 Acting without a license Insurance (ISC) CHAPTER 28, ARTICLE 21 § 2102. Acting without a license. (a) (1) (A) No person, firm, association or corporation shall act as an insurance producer, insurance adjuster or life settlement broker in this state without having authority to do so by virtue of a license issued and in force pursuant to the provisions of this chapter. (B) No person, firm, association or corporation shall act as an excess line broker in this state without having authority to do so by virtue of a license issued and in force pursuant to section two thousand one hundred five of this article, provided, however, that such person, firm, association or corporation shall not be required to be licensed as an excess line broker where the insured's home state is a state other than this state and such person, firm, association or corporation is otherwise licensed to sell, solicit or negotiate excess line insurance in the insured's home state. (2) Any person, firm, association or corporation who or which acts as a reinsurance intermediary in violation of paragraph one hereof shall, in addition to other penalties prescribed by law, be subject to a penalty not to exceed five thousand dollars for each transaction. (b) (1) Unless licensed as an insurance agent, insurance broker or insurance consultant, no person, firm, association or corporation shall in this state identify or hold himself or itself out to be an insurance advisor, insurance consultant or insurance counselor. (2) No person, firm, association or corporation shall use any other designation or title which is likely to mislead the public or shall hold himself or itself out in any manner as having particular insurance qualifications other than those for which he may be otherwise licensed or otherwise qualified. (3) Unless licensed as an insurance agent, insurance broker or insurance consultant with respect to the relevant kinds of insurance, no person, firm, association or corporation shall receive any mon
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies required insurance types and proof of coverage, while the changed text allows CBC to determine adequate coverage based on its internal assessment and offers only a summary of coverage upon request. This contradicts the requirement to maintain specific types and levels of insurance and provide certification, undermining MediWound's protection. The omission of specific insurance types and the certification requirement weakens MediWound's ability to ensure CBC's compliance, creating a contradiction. The snippet from NY Ins. Law § 2102 refers to the need for proper licensing to act as an insurance producer, adjuster, or life settlement broker, implying a regulated framework around insurance activities. While it doesn't directly specify required insurance coverage, it highlights the importance of adherence to insurance regulations, which is undermined by the changed text's discretionary approach to insurance.

perturbation.original_text: 15.1 This Agreement is made under and subject to the provision of the substantive laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.1 This Agreement is made under and subject to the provision of the laws of the Republic of China, without giving effect to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies New York law as the governing law. Changing the governing law to the Republic of China eliminates consumer protections afforded under New York law, potentially violating legally mandated consumer rights applicable if New York law governed.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the governing law, the agreement avoids the application of New York General Business Law § 349, which prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of business. If the agreement involves New York consumers or businesses, this change removes a legal protection they would otherwise have.
perturbation.justification: The change from New York law to the law of the Republic of China directly contradicts the consumer protections offered under New York General Business Law § 349, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also states that it eliminates consumer protections afforded under New York law. The scraped snippet from NYSenate.gov points to Article 22-A, Consumer Protection From Deceptive Acts and Practices, which is a legally mandated consumer protection. Thus, by changing the governing law, the contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, fitting the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15.2 Any disputes relating to this Agreement of whatever nature that cannot be resolved by negotiation between the parties shall be referred for final resolution to arbitration in New York City by 3 (three) Arbitrators under the Rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in English. The decision of the arbitrators shall be final and binding upon the parties and their legal successors. The arbitrators may at their discretion, provide for discovery by the parties not to exceed 4 (four) months from the date of notice of arbitration and the arbitrators shall notify the parties of their decision in writing within 30 (thirty) days of the completion of the final hearing. The arbitrators may at their discretion award costs and expenses in respect of the arbitration.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes relating to this Agreement that cannot be resolved by negotiation between the parties shall be resolved through confidential binding arbitration. The specific arbitration process will be at the sole discretion of CBC.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration in New York City under the American Arbitration Association rules, with specific discovery and notification timelines. The modified text removes these protections and gives CBC sole discretion over the arbitration process. This omission can violate consumer rights if the arbitration clause becomes unfairly biased toward CBC. Given the agreement is under New York Law, the arbitration has to meet some threshold of fairness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the structure and oversight of the American Arbitration Association, potentially creating an unfair arbitration process. While 9 U.S.C. § 2 generally supports arbitration agreements, courts can invalidate them if they are unconscionable or fundamentally unfair. By giving CBC sole discretion, the agreement risks violating this principle and being deemed unenforceable.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies arbitration in New York City under the American Arbitration Association rules, including discovery and notification timelines. The changed text removes these specifications and gives CBC sole discretion over the arbitration process. This omission contradicts the general principle of fairness in arbitration agreements, potentially violating consumer rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 USC 2, supports the validity and enforcement of arbitration agreements; however, the Law Explanation indicates that courts can invalidate agreements that are unconscionable or fundamentally unfair. By granting CBC sole discretion, the revised agreement creates a risk of unfairness, which is a legally recognized basis for invalidating an arbitration agreement, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction.'
